full_notes:
An Imperial Capital Vijayanagara is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 170 An Imperial Capital Vijayanagara ((((( c c c c c. fourteenth to sixteenth century) . fourteenth to sixteenth century) . fourteenth to sixteenth century) . fourteenth to sixteenth century) . fourteenth to sixteenth century) Vijayanagara or “city of victory” was the name of both a city and an empire.

The text explains that the empire was founded in the fourteenth century.

The text explains that in its heyday it stretched from the river Krishna in the north to the extreme south of the peninsula.

The text explains that in 1565 the city was sacked and subsequently deserted.

The text explains that although it fell into ruin in the seventeenth-eighteenth centuries, it lived on in the memories of people living in the Krishna-Tungabhadra doab.

The text explains that they remembered it as Hampi, a name derived from that of the local mother goddess, Pampadevi.

The text explains that these oral traditions combined with archaeological finds, monuments and inscriptions and other records helped scholars to rediscover the Vijayanagara Empire.

The text explains that the Discovery of Hampi The ruins at Hampi were brought to light in 1800 by an engineer and antiquarian named Colonel Colin Mackenzie.

The text explains that an employee of the English East India Company, he prepared the first survey map of the site.

The text explains that much of the initial information he received was based on the memories of priests of the Virupaksha temple and the shrine of Pampadevi.

The text explains that subsequently, from 1856, photographers began to record the monuments which enabled scholars to study them.

The text explains that as early as 1836 epigraphists began collecting several dozen inscriptions found at this and other temples at Hampi.

The text explains that in an effort to reconstruct the history of the city and the empire, historians collated information from these sources with accounts of foreign travellers and other literature written in Telugu, Kannada, Tamil and Sanskrit.

The text explains that 7.1 A part of the stone wall that was built around the city of Vijayanagara THEME SEVEN

The text explains that 171 Colin Mackenzie Born in 1754, Colin Mackenzie became famous as an engineer, surveyor and cartographer.

The text explains that in 1815 he was appointed the first Surveyor General of India, a post he held till his death in 1821.

The text explains that he says that “it struggled long under the miseries of bad management … before the South came under the benign influence of the British government”.

The text explains that on their northern frontier, the Vijayanagara kings competed with contemporary rulers – including the Sultans of the Deccan and the Gajapati rulers of Orissa – for control of the fertile river valleys and the resources generated by lucrative overseas trade.

The text explains that it dates to c.1825 and belongs to the collection of the Royal Asiatic Society of Britain and Ireland.

The text explains that on Mackenzie’s left is his peon Kistnaji holding a telescope, on his right are Brahmana assistants – a Jaina pandit (right) and behind him the Telugu Brahmana Cauvellery Ventak Letchmiah.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 172 Some of the areas that were incorporated within the empire had witnessed the development of powerful states such as those of the Cholas in Tamil Nadu and the Hoysalas in Karnataka.

The text explains that strain began to show within the imperial structure following Krishnadeva Raya’s death in 1529.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 174 Bidar Gulbarga Golconda Warangal Bijapur Ikkeri Quilon Tirunelveli Vijayanagara Chitradurga Kolar Kanchipuram Gingee Chidambaram Thanjavur Madurai Ramanathapuram Chandragiri Mysore Bay of Bengal Indian Ocean Arabian Sea Sri Lanka Bhima Krishna Tungabhadra Pennar Kaveri Vaigai Sketch map not to scale dynasty ruled from Penukonda and later from Chandragiri (near Tirupati).

The text explains that the amara-nayaka system was a major political innovation of the Vijayanagara Empire.

The text explains that it is likely that many features of this system were derived from the iqta system of the Delhi Sultanate.

The text explains that this hastened the collapse of the central imperial structure. Ü Discuss...

The text explains that 7.4 Plan of Vijayanagara Ü Would you find these features in a city today?

The text explains that source 3 Finding out about the city A large number of inscriptions of the kings of Vijayanagara and their nayakas recording donations to temples as well as describing important events have been recovered.

The text explains that 177 3.1 Water resources The most striking feature about the location of Vijayanagara is the natural basin formed by the river Tungabhadra which flows in a north-easterly direction.

The text explains that a number of streams flow down to the river from these rocky outcrops.

The text explains that this canal drew water from a dam across the Tungabhadra and irrigated the cultivated valley that separated the “sacred centre” from the “urban core”.

The text explains that the stone blocks were wedge shaped, which held them in place, and the inner portion of the walls was of earth packed with rubble.

The text explains that in order to make this tank the said king broke down a hill … In the tank I saw so many people at work that there must have been fifteen or twenty thousand men, looking like ants … Source 4 AN IMPERIAL CAPITAL: VIJAYANAGARA

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 178 enter the city there is a great distance, in which are fields in which they sow rice and have many gardens and much water, in which water comes from two lakes.” These statements have been corroborated by present-day archaeologists, who have also found evidence of an agricultural tract between the sacred centre and the urban core.

The text explains that this tract was serviced by an elaborate canal system drawing water from the Tungabhadra.

The text explains that a second line of fortification went round the inner core of the urban complex, and a third line surrounded the royal centre, within which each set of major buildings was surrounded by its own high walls.

The text explains that gateways were distinctive architectural features that often defined the structures to which they regulated access.

The text explains that 7.6) are regarded as typical features of the architecture introduced by the Turkish Sultans.

The text explains that roads generally wound around through the valleys, avoiding rocky terrain.

The text explains that 3.3 The urban core Moving along the roads leading into the urban core, there is relatively little archaeological evidence of the houses of ordinary people.

The text explains that 179 found fine Chinese porcelain in some areas, including in the north-eastern corner of the urban core and suggest that these areas may have been occupied by rich traders.

The text explains that the surveys also indicate that wells, rainwater tanks as well as temple tanks may have served as sources of water to the ordinary town dwellers.

The text explains that these are relatively large structures that do not seem to have been associated Fig.

The text explains that 7.10 A mosque in Vijayanagara Ü What kinds of vessels do you think these shards were originally parts of? Ü Does the mosque have the typical features of Indo-Islamic architecture?

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART II 180 with ritual functions.

The text explains that one difference between these structures and temples is that the latter were constructed entirely of masonry, while the superstructure of the secular buildings was made of perishable materials.

The text explains that 4.1 The mahanavami dibba Some of the more distinctive structures in the area have been assigned names based on the form of the buildings as well as their functions.

The text explains that there is evidence that it supported a wooden structure.

The text explains that rituals associated with the structure probably coincided with Mahanavami (literally, the great ninth day) of the ten-day Hindu festival during the autumn months of September and October, known variously as Dusehra (northern India), Durga Puja (in Bengal) Fig.

The text explains that dances, wrestling matches, and processions of caparisoned horses, elephants and chariots and soldiers, as well as ritual presentations before the king and his guests by the chief nayakas and subordinate kings marked the occasion.

The text explains that trace the routes the travellers mentioned on p.176 would have taken to reach Vijayanagara.

The text explains that find out more about any one of the major cities which flourished in the subcontinent during c. fourteenth-seventeenth centuries.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History II, Chapter 3: An Imperial Capital Vijayanagara
